behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: This is a filtering rule that checks a product field (availability) and excludes matching products. The logic can be verified with a mock catalog containing both in-stock and out-of-stock products at various relevance scores. A pure in-process unit test won't miss the failure mode: a test will either pass (filter works) or fail (out-of-stock products appear).
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that creates a mock catalog with 5 in-stock and 5 out-of-stock products with various relevance scores (including out-of-stock products with higher scores than some in-stock products), runs the recommendation algorithm, and verifies that every result is from an in-stock product.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: This is another filtering rule: exclude products whose category is in user.blocked_categories. Can be tested at Unit level with a mock user object and catalog. The failure is clear: either blocked categories appear (fails) or they don't (passes).
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that creates a user with 3 blocked categories, creates a mock catalog with products in blocked and non-blocked categories, runs the recommendation algorithm, and verifies that zero products from blocked categories appear in the results.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: The ranking logic can be verified at Unit level by providing a user with known browsing history and a catalog with matching and non-matching products, then checking that matching products rank higher. However, Integration adds value if the algorithm uses ML embeddings or if you need to verify that browsing history is retrieved correctly from real storage.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that creates a user with browsing history for 3 products in categories A and B, creates a mock catalog with 10 products (5 in categories A/B, 5 in unrelated categories), runs the recommendation algorithm, and verifies that all top-ranked results are from categories A or B with higher scores than unrelated products.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests two things together: (1) pagination logic is correct, and (2) ranking is deterministic. A Unit test of pagination alone won't catch non-determinism in the ranking algorithm. Real failure: requesting page 2 twice yields different product sets because ranking is non-deterministic (random tiebreaking, set iteration order, external factor). Must run the actual recommendation endpoint twice to catch this.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that requests page 2 of recommendations for a user with known catalog and browsing history, records the exact set and order of products, immediately requests page 2 again with no intervening changes, and verifies both responses contain identical products in identical positions.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: The serendipity logic can be tested at Unit level by creating a user with browsing history in a subset of categories, providing a catalog with products in new categories, and verifying at least one result is from an unbrowsed category. Mocking both the user history and catalog is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that creates a user with browsing history in categories A and B only, creates a mock catalog with products in categories A, B, and C (where C is new), runs the recommendation algorithm requesting 5+ results, and verifies that at least one result is from category C.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: The cold-start fallback logic can be tested at Unit level with a mock user object (empty history), mock catalog with popularity scores, and verification that results are non-empty and ordered by popularity. No external dependency needed.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test that creates a new user with empty purchase_history and browsing_history, creates a mock catalog where products have explicit popularity_score attributes, runs the recommendation algorithm, and verifies results are non-empty and ranked in descending order of popularity.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Mocking the database insert call hides actual persistence bugs. Real failure: clicks are dropped silently, or recorded with wrong product ID/position/timestamp because data isn't being persisted or schema is wrong. Must test with a real test database to verify clicks are actually written with correct schema.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that generates a recommendation (recording the exact product ID and position), simulates a user click on that product, queries the test database's click-tracking collection, and verifies that exactly one click record exists with the correct product_id, position, and timestamp (within 2 seconds of the click).

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA: Must test with realistic scale (100K products) to catch algorithmic bottlenecks. Unit tests on small datasets won't trigger performance issues. Need a test with actual 100K product scale to measure end-to-end latency. System level relevant if algorithm calls external APIs, but Integration with realistic data is minimum.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that populates a test catalog with 100,000 realistic mock products, makes 10 recommendation requests for a user with typical browsing history, measures each response time, and verifies all responses complete in under 200ms.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-HIDE: Requires verifying that the algorithm's output actually changes after a purchase event. Mocking both purchase recording and ranking would test mocks, not the system. Real failure: purchase is recorded but algorithm ignores it and still ranks the product high. Must record purchase in persistent state and run the algorithm again to compare outputs.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that generates a recommendation list for a user and records where product X appears (or that it doesn't), records a purchase of product X in the database, generates a new recommendation list for the same user, and verifies that product X is either absent or ranked significantly lower than before.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: Real failure: requesting recommendations twice yields different ranked lists (non-deterministic). Could be from: randomness in algorithm, set iteration order, floating-point precision, or external factors. Must run the actual algorithm twice with identical state to detect this. Unit test of individual components won't catch this system-level non-determinism.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that makes a recommendation request for a user with fixed state, records the complete ranked list (product IDs and order), makes an identical recommendation request with no intervening activity, and verifies the two lists are byte-identical in product order.
